questions.text: The oxygen and carbon dioxide crosses the plasma membrane by the process of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Active diffusion, also known as active transport, is a process that requires energy (usually in the form of ATP) to move substances against their concentration gradient. This process is not involved in the movement of oxygen and carbon dioxide across the Plasma membrane.

Facilitated diffusion is a passive process that involves the movement of molecules or ions across a membrane through a protein channel or carrier molecule. Oxygen and carbon dioxide move across the Plasma membrane by Facilitated diffusion through specific membrane proteins.

Passive diffusion, also known as simple diffusion, is a process by which molecules move across a membrane from an area of higher concentration to an area of lower concentration. This process is responsible for the movement of oxygen and carbon dioxide across the Plasma membrane.

Random diffusion is not a well-defined process but may refer to the movement of particles or molecules due to random thermal motion. While they may contribute to the overall movement of gases across the Plasma membrane, it is not the primary process involved.

questions.explanation: Passive diffusion, also known as simple diffusion, is a process by which molecules move across a membrane from an area of higher concentration to an area of lower concentration. This process is responsible for the movement of oxygen and carbon dioxide across the Plasma membrane.

questions.text: What is the microscope's ability to distinguish between separate objects that are close together?

Contrast refers to the degree of difference between the light and dark regions of an image.

Resolving power or resolution is the ability to distinguish objects that are close together. The better the resolving power of the microscope, the closer together two objects can be and still be seen as separate.
Scanning power is not a commonly used term in microscopy, but it may refer to the ability of a microscope to scan a large area, such as in a scanning electron microscope.

questions.explanation: Resolving power or resolution is the ability to distinguish objects that are close together. The better the resolving power of the microscope, the closer together two objects can be and still be seen as separate.

questions.text: The fluidity of the plasma membrane increases with which of the following?

questions.text: Cellular organelles that interact with hydrogen peroxide are called?

options.explanation: Plants contain an organelle, which in addition to glycolic acid oxidase and catalase also possess a number of enzymes that are not found in animal cells. These organelles, called glyoxysomes are most abundant in plant seedlings, which rely upon stored fatty acids to provide them with the energy and material to begin the formation of a new plant. One of the primary activities in these germinating seedlings is the conversion of stored fatty acids to carbohydrates. This is achieved through a cycle, glyoxylate cycle, the enzymes of which are located in the glyoxysomes.

Lysosomes are cytoplasmic organelles and are different from others due to their morphology. These were isolated as a separate components for the first time by De Duve (1949). Lysosomes (Lyso = splitting; soma = body) are found in most eukaryotic cells.

The cell contains many tiny granular structures known as ribosomes. Palade (1955) was the first person to study them. Eukaryotic ribosomes are composed of an almost equal amount of RNA and protein, hence they are ribonucleoprotein particles. The RNA present in ribosomes is called ribosomal RNA.

This option is correct because De-Duve and coworkers isolated 1965 particles from liver cells and other tissues which were enriched with some oxidative enzymes, such as peroxidase, catalase, glycolic acid oxidase, and some other enzymes. The name peroxisome was applied because this organelle is specifically involved in the formation and decomposition of hydrogen peroxide in the cell.

questions.explanation: De-Duve and coworkers isolated 1965 particles from liver cells and other tissues that were enriched with some oxidative enzymes, such as peroxidase, catalase, glycolic acid oxidase, and some other enzymes. The name peroxisome was applied because this organelle is specifically involved in the formation and decomposition of hydrogen peroxide in the cell. These are single membrane-enclosed cytoplasmic organelles found both in animal and plant cells. These are characterized by containing H202-producing oxidases and catalase. They are approximately 0.5 nm in diameter. They have also been found in protozoa, yeast, and many cell types of higher plants.

questions.text: Shape of the maturing phase of the golgi apparatus is?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A biconcave shape refers to a structure that is concave on both sides, resembling a hollowed-out disk or lens. This shape is not typically associated with the maturing phase of the Golgi apparatus.
Convex shape refers to a structure that is curved outward, resembling a bulge or a dome. This shape is also not characteristic of the maturing phase of the Golgi apparatus.

A spherical shape refers to a structure that is perfectly round and symmetrical, resembling a ball or a sphere. The Golgi apparatus does not have a spherical shape during its maturing phase. Instead, it consists of stacked, curved cisternae.

A concave shape refers to a structure that is curved inward, with a surface that is depressed or hollowed out. This shape is associated with the maturing phase of the Golgi apparatus. The cisternae within the Golgi stack are curved and form a concave shape as proteins and lipids move through the organelle.

questions.text: Pancreas produces secretory granules that help in digestion. These granules, after passing through Endoplasmic reticulum, are pinched off from _ surface of Golgi apparatus?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The forming phase refers to the early stages of vesicle formation in the Golgi apparatus. During this phase, secretory granules and other vesicles are budding off from the Golgi membrane. This phase involves the initial packaging and sorting of proteins and other molecules within the Golgi apparatus.

The maturing phase of the Golgi apparatus is when the secretory granules become fully formed and ready for secretion. During this phase, further processing, sorting, and modification of proteins and other molecules occur within the Golgi apparatus. The maturing phase is characterized by the final packaging and sorting of these molecules before they are transported to their target destinations.

The cis-end of the Golgi apparatus is the entry or receiving side. It is where materials from the endoplasmic reticulum are received and processed. At the cis-end, proteins and other molecules are modified and sorted for further processing within the Golgi apparatus.

questions.text: What happens if golgi apparatus is removed from the cell?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: All sorts of vesicle formation stops
options.explanation: The Golgi apparatus is involved in the formation of various types of vesicles, including transport vesicles that carry proteins, lipids, and other molecules to their intended destinations within the cell or for secretion outside the cell. Without the Golgi apparatus, vesicle formation would be severely impaired.

options.text: All sorts of protein synthesis stops
options.explanation: While the Golgi apparatus is involved in the processing and modification of proteins, it is not directly responsible for protein synthesis. Protein synthesis primarily occurs in the ribosomes, which are located in the cytoplasm or attached to the endoplasmic reticulum (ER).

options.text: All sorts of fat synthesis stops
options.explanation: The Golgi apparatus is not directly involved in fat synthesis. Lipid synthesis primarily takes place in the ER and other cellular compartments, while the Golgi apparatus mainly processes and modifies lipids.

options.text: All sorts of enzyme synthesis stops
options.explanation: The Golgi apparatus is involved in the modification and processing of enzymes, but it is not responsible for enzyme synthesis. Enzyme synthesis primarily occurs in the ribosomes and is facilitated by the genetic information encoded in the DNA.

questions.explanation: The Golgi apparatus is involved in the formation of various types of vesicles, including transport vesicles that carry proteins, lipids, and other molecules to their intended destinations within the cell or for secretion outside the cell. Without the Golgi apparatus, vesicle formation would be severely impaired.

questions.text: Which of the following is left behind when a plant cell is treated by cellulase and pectinase enzymes?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Chloroplasts are specialized organelles found in plant cells that are responsible for photosynthesis. They contain chlorophyll, a pigment that captures sunlight and converts it into chemical energy. When a plant cell is treated with cellulase and pectinase enzymes, the chloroplasts are not specifically targeted, and they generally remain intact unless additional enzymes or treatments are used to disrupt them.

Leucoplasts are colorless organelles found in plant cells that are involved in storing and synthesizing various substances, such as starch, oils, or pigments. They are not directly related to photosynthesis like chloroplasts. When a plant cell is treated with cellulase and pectinase enzymes, leucoplasts are not specifically targeted, and they typically remain intact unless additional enzymes or treatments are used to disrupt them.

The protoplast refers to the living part of a plant cell, which includes the cytoplasm, nucleus, and other organelles such as mitochondria, endoplasmic reticulum, and Golgi apparatus. It does not include the cell wall or any plastids. When a plant cell is treated with cellulase and pectinase enzymes, these enzymes break down the cell wall, releasing the protoplast.

Chromoplasts are organelles found in some plant cells that contain pigments other than chlorophyll, such as carotenoids, which give fruits and flowers their vibrant colors. Chromoplasts are not directly involved in photosynthesis. When a plant cell is treated with cellulase and pectinase enzymes, chromoplasts are not specifically targeted, and they typically remain intact unless additional enzymes or treatments are used to disrupt them.

questions.explanation: The protoplast refers to the living part of a plant cell, which includes the cytoplasm, nucleus, and other organelles such as mitochondria, endoplasmic reticulum, and Golgi apparatus. It does not include the cell wall or any plastids. When a plant cell is treated with cellulase and pectinase enzymes, these enzymes break down the cell wall, releasing the protoplast.

questions.text: Which one of the following is a non-protoplasmic cell inclusion?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Ribosomes are small organelles found in all cells. They play a crucial role in protein synthesis by translating messenger RNA (mRNA) into proteins. Ribosomes are composed of RNA and proteins and are considered to be part of the protoplasm.

Mitochondria are double-membraned organelles found in most eukaryotic cells. They are often referred to as the “powerhouses'' of the cell because they generate energy in the form of adenosine triphosphate (ATP) through the process of cellular respiration. Mitochondria have their own DNA and are considered to be part of the protoplasm.

Lysosomes are membrane-bound organelles found in animal cells. They contain various enzymes that are involved in the breakdown of macromolecules, cellular waste, and foreign substances through a process called hydrolysis. Lysosomes are considered to be part of the protoplasm.

Cystoliths are non-protoplasmic cell inclusions found in certain plant cells. They are calcareous bodies composed of calcium carbonate. Cystoliths are not considered to be part of the protoplasm but rather are found in the extracellular space within specific plant tissues, such as leaves and stems.

questions.explanation: Cystoliths are non-protoplasmic cell inclusions found in certain plant cells. They are calcareous bodies composed of calcium carbonate. questions.explanation: The egg and sperm cells, the reproductive cells or gametes, are haploid, meaning they contain only one set of chromosomes. In humans, the haploid number of chromosomes is 23.During meiosis, the process of cell division that produces gametes, the parent cell undergoes two rounds of cell division to produce four daughter cells, each with half the number of chromosomes as the parent cell. This ensures that when the egg and sperm fuse during fertilization, the resulting zygote will have the usual diploid number of chromosomes, which is 46 in humans.

questions.text: Resolution of human eye is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The resolution of the human eye is commonly cited to be around 0.1 millimeters (100 micrometers) rather than 2.0 micrometers. The resolution refers to the ability of the eye to distinguish fine details, and it can vary among individuals.

Actually, the resolution of the human eye is not typically measured in millimeters. It's commonly referred to in terms of angular resolution, which is expressed in arcminutes or arcseconds. The human eye's angular resolution is around 1 arcminute, which means it can distinguish two points that are about 1/60th of a degree apart.

The resolution of the human eye is not 1.0 um (micrometer). The average resolution of the human eye is approximately 0.02 to 0.04 degrees, which corresponds to about 1 arcminute or 60 arcseconds. This translates to a spatial resolution of around 0.3 to 0.6 arcminutes per line pair, which is roughly equivalent to 0.6 to 1.2 arcseconds per line pair. In terms of distance, this corresponds to distinguishing objects that are about 0.6 to 1.2 arcseconds apart.

Resolution refers to the ability of an imaging system to separate or distinguish between two closely spaced objects. The resolution of the human eye is approximately 0.1 mm or 100 micrometers. This means that two objects need to be at least 0.1 mm apart to be perceived as distinct by the human eye.

questions.explanation: Resolution refers to the ability of an imaging system to separate or distinguish between two closely spaced objects. The resolution of the human eye is approximately 0.1 mm or 100 micrometers. This means that two objects need to be at least 0.1 mm apart to be perceived as distinct by the human eye.

questions.text: Which of the following are assembled and disassembled during the cell cycle?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Microtubules are dynamic structures that are assembled and disassembled during the cell cycle.
Microfilaments, also known as actin filaments, are not assembled and disassembled during the cell cycle.
This option is incorrect.
This option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The cell cycle is the process by which a cell grows, duplicates its genetic material, and divides into two daughter cells. It consists of four main stages: G1 (Gap 1), S (Synthesis), G2 (Gap 2), and M (Mitosis). During G1, the cell prepares for DNA replication. In the S phase, DNA synthesis occurs, resulting in two identical copies of the cell's DNA. G2 is a preparation phase before cell division. Finally, during the M phase, the cell divides into two daughter cells through either mitosis (for somatic cells) or meiosis (for reproductive cells). Microtubules are dynamic structures that are assembled and disassembled during the cell cycle. They are involved in a variety of cellular processes including chromosome segregation during mitosis and meiosis, intracellular transport of organelles and vesicles, and maintenance of cell shape and polarity. The assembly and disassembly of microtubules are tightly regulated by a variety of cellular factors and play critical roles in maintaining cellular homeostasis. Microfilaments and intermediate filaments are also part of the cytoskeleton, but they do not undergo dynamic assembly and disassembly during the cell cycle.

questions.text: Which substances can cross plasma membrane more easily?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Starch is a large, polar molecule that cannot dissolve in the fatty acids of the plasma membrane. Therefore, it cannot cross the plasma membrane easily. Starch is a major component of plant cells and is used to store energy.

Protein is also a large, polar molecule that cannot dissolve in the fatty acids of the plasma membrane. However, some proteins can cross the plasma membrane with the help of special proteins called transporters. Transporters are specific for certain proteins and help them to cross the membrane.

Lipid soluble molecules can dissolve in the fatty acids of the plasma membrane and cross the membrane easily. This is because they are small and nonpolar, which means that they do not have any electrical charge. Some examples of lipid soluble molecules include oxygen, carbon dioxide, and fat-soluble vitamins.

Ions are charged molecules that cannot dissolve in the fatty acids of the plasma membrane. However, some ions can cross the plasma membrane with the help of special proteins called channels. Channels are open pores that allow ions to pass through the membrane.

questions.explanation: Lipid soluble molecules can cross plasma more easily than starch, protein, and ions. The plasma membrane is a phospholipid bilayer, which is made up of two layers of fatty acids. Lipid soluble molecules, such as oxygen, carbon dioxide, and fat-soluble vitamins, can dissolve in the fatty acids of the plasma membrane and cross the membrane easily.

questions.text: Why are phospholipids a major part of the lipid bilayer in plasma membranes?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: They have a nitrogenous base in the head region
options.explanation: Phospholipids do not have a nitrogenous base in their head region. Instead, the head region of a phospholipid contains a phosphate group, which is polar (hydrophilic).

options.text: They have fatty acids in the tail region
options.explanation: This option is partially correct. Phospholipids consist of two fatty acid chains in their tail region. These fatty acid chains are nonpolar (hydrophobic) and repel water, making them an essential part of the lipid bilayer.

Phospholipids are amphipathic molecules. This means that they have a hydrophilic, polar phosphate head and two hydrophobic fatty acid tails. These components of the phospholipids cause them to orientate themselves.

options.text: They have a phosphate group in the head region
options.explanation: As mentioned earlier, the head region of a phospholipid contains a phosphate group, which is negatively charged and polar. This phosphate group contributes to the hydrophilic nature of the head, enabling the formation of hydrogen bonds with water molecules.

questions.explanation: In summary, the major reason why phospholipids are a crucial part of the lipid bilayer in plasma membranes is their amphipathic nature (option c). The hydrophilic head with the phosphate group interacts with water, while the hydrophobic tails with fatty acids cluster together away from water, forming a stable and functional barrier that defines the plasma membrane's structure and function. Phospholipids are amphipathic molecules. This means that they have a hydrophilic, polar phosphate head and two hydrophobic fatty acid tails. These components of the phospholipids cause them to orientate themselves.

questions.text: Enzymes involved in protein synthesis are most likely to be associated with which cell organelle?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The rough endoplasmic reticulum (RER) is an organelle involved in protein synthesis. It has ribosomes attached to its surface, which are responsible for translating mRNA and synthesizing proteins. These proteins are often destined for secretion, incorporation into the cell membrane, or transport to other organelles.

The Golgi complex is another organelle involved in protein synthesis. After proteins are synthesized on the ribosomes of the rough endoplasmic reticulum, they are packaged into transport vesicles and sent to the Golgi complex. Here, the proteins undergo post-translational modifications, such as glycosylation or phosphorylation, and are sorted and directed to their final destinations within the cell.

Ribosomes are small structures found either floating freely in the cytoplasm (free ribosomes) or bound to the rough endoplasmic reticulum (membrane-bound ribosomes). Ribosomes are the actual site of protein synthesis, where the mRNA sequence is translated into a polypeptide chain.

options.text: All of the above options are correct
options.explanation: This statement is true. All the options listed (rough endoplasmic reticulum, Golgi complex, and ribosomes) are involved in protein synthesis in their respective ways. The rough endoplasmic reticulum provides the site for initial protein synthesis, the Golgi complex processes and modifies the proteins, and ribosomes are the essential machinery where the actual synthesis of proteins occurs.

questions.explanation: This statement is true. All the options listed (rough endoplasmic reticulum, golgi complex, and ribosomes) are involved in protein synthesis in their respective ways. The rough endoplasmic reticulum provides the site for initial protein synthesis, the golgi complex processes and modifies the proteins, and ribosomes are the essential machinery where the actual synthesis of proteins occurs.

questions.text: How does the human sperm locomote?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Human sperm locomotion is primarily achieved through the movement of flagella. A flagellum is a long, whip-like appendage that extends from the head of the sperm. It contains a complex arrangement of microtubules that enable it to move in a whip-like motion, propelling the sperm forward through the fluid environment of the female reproductive tract. This movement allows the sperm to swim towards the egg and increases the chances of successful fertilization.

Cilia are hair-like structures found in various cells and tissues of the human body, but they are not involved in the locomotion of human sperm. Cilia have different functions depending on their location in the body. For example, cilia in the respiratory tract help to sweep mucus and foreign particles away from the lungs, while cilia in the fallopian tubes help to move the egg towards the uterus.
Pilli (or Pili) are hair-like appendages found on the surface of some bacteria. They are not involved in human sperm locomotion.

options.text: All of these options are correct
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because only option a) Flagella is correct. Sperm locomotion is achieved through the movement of flagella, and cilia and pilli are not involved in this process in human sperm.

questions.explanation: The tail of the sperm contains the flagellum that confers motility upon the sperm. Thus, human sperm locomotes with the help of flagella that get energy from mitochondria.

questions.text: What is the part of the cell membrane which is in contact with the internal and external environments?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The hydrophobic property refers to substances or regions of molecules that repel or are insoluble in water. In the context of the cell membrane, the interior of the lipid bilayer is composed of hydrophobic fatty acid tails of phospholipids. This hydrophobic region forms a barrier to the passage of water-soluble molecules, helping to separate the intracellular and extracellular environments.

The hydrophilic property refers to substances or regions of molecules that have an affinity for water. In the context of the cell membrane, the hydrophilic heads of phospholipids face both the internal and external environments of the cell, where they come into contact with the aqueous surroundings. Additionally, the hydrophilic heads of phospholipids are also found on the outer surface of the membrane.

The term "neutral" does not accurately describe the property of the part of the cell membrane in contact with the internal and external environments. The cell membrane has distinct regions with different properties, as explained by the hydrophobic and hydrophilic regions described above.

options.text: Both Options A and B are correct
options.explanation: This option is correct. The cell membrane is composed of a lipid bilayer, with the hydrophobic tails pointing inward, away from the aqueous environments, and the hydrophilic heads facing outward, in contact with the internal and external environments of the cell. Both properties, hydrophobic and hydrophilic, are essential for maintaining the integrity and functionality of the cell membrane.

questions.text: The image represented by a compound microscope is:

options.explanation: A real image is an image that can be projected onto a screen. A compound microscope produces a real image, but it is also inverted.
A virtual inverted image is an image that is upside down and cannot be projected onto a screen. A compound microscope produces a real inverted image, not a virtual inverted image.

A virtual image is an image that cannot be projected onto a screen. It is formed when light rays appear to converge at a point, but they do not actually meet at that point. A compound microscope produces a real image, not a virtual image.

This option is correct because the final image produced by a compound microscope is a real inverted image. The image is real because it is formed by the objective lens, which is a converging lens. The image is inverted because the object is viewed through two converging lenses.

questions.explanation: The image represented by a compound microscope is real and inverted. A compound microscope consists of two lenses: the objective lens and the eyepiece. The objective lens is the lens that is closest to the object being viewed. It is responsible for producing the initial image of the object. The eyepiece is the lens that is closest to the viewer's eye. It is responsible for magnifying the image produced by the objective lens. Therefore, the final image produced by a compound microscope is a real inverted image. The image is real because it is formed by the objective lens, which is a converging lens. The image is inverted because the object is viewed through two converging lenses.

questions.text: The cells that lack lysosomes would have difficulty in which of the following?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The correct statement is that a cell lacking lysozyme would have difficulty digesting food. Lysozyme's role in breaking down bacterial cell walls is crucial for the digestion of food, particularly in protecting against bacterial infections. However, it is not directly involved in energy storage, protein packaging, or cytoplasmic movement.

Lysosomes are membrane-bound organelles that contain enzymes responsible for breaking down macromolecules, including proteins, nucleic acids, carbohydrates, and lipids. They are not directly involved in the storage of energy.

Protein packaging is primarily performed by the Golgi apparatus, which modifies, sorts, and packages proteins for transport to their appropriate destinations. While lysosomes are involved in intracellular digestion and recycling of cellular components, they are not directly responsible for protein packaging.

The movement of cytoplasm within cells is primarily facilitated by the cytoskeleton, including microtubules, actin filaments, and intermediate filaments. Lysosomes are not directly involved in the process of moving cytoplasm.

questions.explanation: The lysosome is a membrane-bound cell organelle that contains digestive enzymes. Hence, cells that lack lysosomes would have difficulty digesting food.

questions.text: Which of the following imparts a red color to Rose petals?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Chloroplasts are organelles found in plant cells that are responsible for photosynthesis. They contain chlorophyll, which gives plants their green color and is essential for capturing sunlight and converting it into chemical energy. However, chloroplasts do not impart a red color to rose flowers.

Chlorophyll is a pigment found in chloroplasts that absorbs light energy for photosynthesis. It is primarily responsible for the green color of plants. While chlorophyll is present in rose leaves and stems, it is not responsible for the red color of rose flowers.

Chromoplasts are plastids responsible for synthesizing and storing pigments other than chlorophyll. They produce a wide range of colors, including red, orange, and yellow pigments. Chromoplasts are particularly abundant in plant structures such as petals, fruits, and vegetables, where they contribute to the vibrant hues that attract pollinators or aid in seed dispersal.

Leucoplasts are colorless plastids found in plant cells. They are involved in various metabolic processes, such as the synthesis and storage of starch, oils, and proteins. Leucoplasts do not contain pigments that can impart a red color to rose flowers.

questions.explanation: Chromoplasts are plastids, heterogeneous organelles responsible for pigment synthesis (storage and synthesis of carotenoid pigments in flowers and fruits).

questions.text: Who opposed the idea the cell is an empty space bounded by thick wall?

options.explanation: Lorenz Oken was a German naturalist and biologist who made significant contributions to the fields of natural history and comparative anatomy. He is not particularly known for opposing the idea of the cell structure.

Theodor Schwann was a German physiologist and one of the founders of the cell theory. He, along with Matthias Schleiden, proposed that all living organisms are composed of cells and that the cell is the basic unit of life. Schwann opposed the earlier idea that the cell is an empty space bounded by a thick wall and instead suggested that cells have a distinct structure and organization.

Robert Brown was a Scottish botanist who is famous for discovering the random movement of microscopic particles in a fluid, known as "Brownian motion." Robert Brown discovered that cell is not an empty space but inside the cell there is a large nucleus or many nuclei depending upon the cells

Rudolph Virchow was a German physician, pathologist, and biologist who is considered one of the founders of modern cellular pathology. He is best known for his statement, "Omnis cellula e cellula," which means "every cell originates from another existing cell.

questions.explanation: Robert Brown discovered that cell is not an empty space but inside the cell there is a large nucleus or many nuclei depending upon the cells

This option suggests that the image is both real (able to be projected) and inverted (upside down). While some optical systems, like telescopes, can produce real and inverted images, a compound microscope primarily produces virtual images that are not inverted.

questions.text: Transverse diffusion (flip-flop) is the movement of which of the following molecules?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: It refers to cholesterol, which is a type of lipid present in the cell membrane. Cholesterol molecules can move within the plane of the cell membrane through a process called lateral diffusion, but they are not known to undergo transverse diffusion (flip-flop). Transverse diffusion involves the movement of molecules between the two leaflets (layers) of the lipid bilayer, and cholesterol does not typically flip-flop in this manner.

It refers to amino acids, which are the building blocks of proteins. Amino acids are not directly involved in the structure of the cell membrane or the lipid bilayer. Therefore, they do not undergo transverse diffusion (flip-flop) in the cell membrane.

It refers to proteins, which are large molecules with diverse functions in the cell. While proteins can interact with the lipid bilayer and move laterally within the membrane, they are generally too large to undergo transverse diffusion (flip-flop) across the lipid bilayer.
Phospholipids are the main components of the cell membrane and form a lipid bilayer with hydrophilic heads facing outward and hydrophobic tails facing inward.

questions.explanation: Transverse diffusion or flip-flop involves the movement of lipid or protein from one membrane surface to the other .

questions.text: Fatty acids move through the plasma membrane by which transport method?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is correct because Fatty acid transport across the plasma membrane is a type of passive transport. Passive transport is the movement of molecules across the plasma membrane without the expenditure of energy. Fatty acids can move across the plasma membrane by both passive diffusion and facilitated transport.

This term is not commonly used in the context of cellular transport mechanisms. In general, transport mechanisms are described as either passive (requiring no energy, like simple diffusion) or active (requiring energy input). "Non-facilitated transport" does not describe a recognized transport mechanism.

Active transport involves the movement of molecules against their concentration gradient, from an area of lower concentration to an area of higher concentration. This process requires the input of energy, typically in the form of ATP, and often involves specific membrane transport proteins, such as pumps or carriers. Active transport is necessary for the movement of certain ions and molecules when they need to be concentrated on one side of the membrane, which is not the primary method for moving fatty acids.

Facilitated transport is a process in which specific transport proteins (carriers or channels) help molecules move down their concentration gradient. This process does not require energy input and is often used for the movement of polar or larger molecules.

questions.explanation: Fatty acid transport across the plasma membrane is a type of passive transport. Passive transport is the movement of molecules across the plasma membrane without the expenditure of energy. Fatty acids can move across the plasma membrane by both passive diffusion and facilitated transport. Passive diffusion is the movement of molecules from an area of high concentration to an area of low concentration. Fatty acids can passively diffuse across the plasma membrane because they are hydrophobic molecules. Hydrophobic molecules dissolve in lipids, and the phospholipid bilayer of the plasma membrane is made up of lipids. Therefore, fatty acids can dissolve in the phospholipid bilayer and diffuse across the membrane.The substances which are lipid soluble cross it more easily than others, therefore, it regulates the low of materials and ions to maintain a definite gradient. Many small gas molecules, water, glucose, etc. being neutral can easily cross while ions, being charged particles, have some difficulty in crossing. Many substances which are not needed, constantly enter the cell by passive transport.

questions.text: The organelle serving as a primary packaging area for molecules that will be distributed throughout the cell is?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A vacuole is a membrane-bound organelle that is found in the cells of many eukaryotic organisms. Vacuoles are essentially sacs filled with fluid, and they play a variety of roles depending on the type of cell in which they are found. Vacuoles are an important adaptation that allows cells to maintain their structure and function, and they play a crucial role in many cellular processes.

Plastids are a group of membrane-bound organelles that are found in the cells of plants and algae. Plastids are responsible for a variety of functions, including photosynthesis, storage of starches and lipids, and the synthesis of pigments and other important compounds.

Lysosomes are membrane-bound organelles that are found in the cells of most eukaryotic organisms. Lysosomes contain a variety of enzymes that are responsible for breaking down and recycling cellular waste products and macromolecules, such as proteins, nucleic acids, and lipids.

The Golgi apparatus, or Golgi complex, is the organelle that serves as the primary packing and distribution centre in eukaryotic cells. The Golgi apparatus is responsible for sorting, modifying, and packaging proteins and lipids that are synthesized in the endoplasmic reticulum (ER) and preparing them for transport to their final destinations within the cell or for secretion outside of the cell.

questions.explanation: The Golgi apparatus, or Golgi complex, is the organelle that serves as the primary packing and distribution center in eukaryotic cells. The Golgi apparatus is responsible for sorting, modifying, and packaging proteins and lipids that are synthesized in the endoplasmic reticulum (ER) and preparing them for transport to their final destinations within the cell or for secretion outside of the cell. The Golgi apparatus consists of a series of flattened, membrane-bound sacs called cisternae, which are organized into several distinct regions based on their function. The Golgi apparatus is an essential organelle that plays a crucial role in maintaining the structure and function of eukaryotic cells.

questions.text: The isolation of different cellular components to determine their chemical composition can be achieved by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cell differentiation is a process by which unspecialized cells become specialized in structure and function. It does not involve the isolation of different cellular components to determine their chemical composition. Cell fractionation is the technique used to isolate different cellular components for the purpose of studying their chemical composition, which is different from cell differentiation.

Chromatography is a technique used to separate and isolate different molecules based on their physical and chemical properties. It is often used in biochemistry to separate and isolate different cellular components, such as proteins, nucleic acids, and lipids, which can then be analyzed to determine their chemical composition.

The isolation of different cellular components to determine their chemical composition can be achieved by a technique called cell fractionation. Cell fractionation involves breaking open cells and separating their components based on size, shape, and density. This can be done using a variety of different methods, such as centrifugation, differential sedimentation, and chromatography. By separating the different cellular components, scientists can study their chemical composition and determine their functions within the cell.
This option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The plasma membrane is made up of a phospholipid bilayer, which is a double layer of phospholipids. Phospholipids have a hydrophilic head and a hydrophobic tail. The hydrophilic heads face the outside and inside of the cell, while the hydrophobic tails face each other. The hydrophobic tails of the lipid bilayer create a barrier that prevents ions from crossing the plasma membrane. Ions are charged particles, and they are attracted to water. The hydrophobic tails of the lipid bilayer are repelled by water, so ions cannot cross through them. Channel proteins are proteins that span the plasma membrane and create pores through which ions can pass. Channel proteins are selective, so they only allow certain types of ions to pass through.

questions.text: Which statement describes an incorrect difference between a plant cell and a bacterial cell?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Bacterial cell has 70S ribosomes whereas a plant cell has 80S ribosomes
options.explanation: The statement "Bacterial cell has 70S ribosomes whereas a plant cell has 80S ribosomes" is true. Bacterial cells typically have 70S ribosomes, which are smaller and less complex than the 80S ribosomes found in eukaryotic cells like plant cells.

options.text: Bacterial cell divides by binary fission whereas a plant cell divides by mitosis
options.explanation: The statement "Bacterial cell divides by binary fission whereas a plant cell divides by mitosis" is also true. Bacterial cells reproduce through binary fission, a process where the cell divides into two identical daughter cells. On the other hand, plant cells and other eukaryotic cells divide through mitosis, a more complex process involving the division and distribution of genetic material into two daughter cells.

options.text: Bacterial cells do not have a nuclear membrane whereas plant cells do
options.explanation: The statement "Bacterial cells do not have a nuclear membrane whereas plant cells do" is true as well. Bacterial cells are prokaryotic, meaning they lack a true nucleus and other membrane-bound organelles. Plant cells, being eukaryotic, do have a distinct nucleus enclosed within a nuclear membrane.

options.text: None of these options are correct
options.explanation: As all the above statements are true, so option D is correct.

questions.explanation: The statement "Bacterial cell has 70S ribosomes whereas a plant cell has 80S ribosomes" is true. Bacterial cells typically have 70S ribosomes, which are smaller and less complex than the 80S ribosomes found in eukaryotic cells like plant cells. The statement "Bacterial cell divides by binary fission whereas a plant cell divides by mitosis" is also true. Bacterial cells reproduce through binary fission, a process where the cell divides into two identical daughter cells. On the other hand, plant cells and other eukaryotic cells divide through mitosis, a more complex process involving the division and distribution of genetic material into two daughter cells. The statement "Bacterial cells do not have a nuclear membrane whereas plant cells do" is true as well. Bacterial cells are prokaryotic, meaning they lack a true nucleus and other membrane-bound organelles. Plant cells, being eukaryotic, do have a distinct nucleus enclosed within a nuclear membrane. So, none of the given statements describes an incorrect difference between a plant cell and a bacterial cell.

questions.text: The factory of ribosomal synthesis is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The cytoplasm is the gel-like substance that fills the cell and surrounds the cell organelles. While ribosomes are found in the cytoplasm and are involved in protein synthesis, the cytoplasm itself is not the factory where ribosomes are synthesized. Ribosomes are synthesized elsewhere in the cell before they are transported to the cytoplasm for protein synthesis.

The nucleus is a membrane-bound organelle found in eukaryotic cells that contains the cell's genetic material, including DNA. The nucleus controls cell activities and serves as the control center of the cell. While the nucleus is involved in gene expression and controls the synthesis of ribosomal RNA (rRNA), it is not the primary location where ribosomal subunits are assembled to form functional ribosomes.

The nucleolus is a substructure within the nucleus of eukaryotic cells, and it is the primary site of ribosomal RNA (rRNA) transcription and ribosome assembly. It is the correct answer because it is the factory of ribosomal synthesis. The nucleolus synthesizes and assembles the ribosomal subunits, which are later exported to the cytoplasm for final assembly into functional ribosomes.

The endoplasmic reticulum (ER) is a complex network of membranes found in eukaryotic cells involved in various cellular processes, such as protein synthesis, lipid synthesis, and detoxification. While the ER plays a role in protein synthesis, it is not the primary site of ribosomal synthesis. Ribosomal synthesis mainly occurs in the nucleolus within the nucleus.

questions.explanation: The nucleolus acts as a site for the synthesis of ribosomes.

questions.text: What is not true about liposomes?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: They are lipid vesicles with their polar heads of phospholipids on the inner and hydrophobic tails on the outer side
options.explanation: This statement is not true. Liposomes are lipid vesicles with their polar heads of phospholipids facing outward towards the aqueous environment, and their hydrophobic tails facing inward, forming a bilayer structure. The correct orientation is polar heads on the outer side and hydrophobic tails on the inner side.
This statement is true. Liposomes are bilayered spherical structures composed of lipid molecules. The lipid bilayer forms a spherical vesicle with an aqueous core.

options.text: They can easily cross the lipid bilayer
options.explanation: This statement is true. So this option is incorrect.

options.text: They can be used in drug delivery system
options.explanation: This statement is true. Liposomes are widely used in drug delivery systems. They can encapsulate drugs, protecting them from degradation and improving their bioavailability. Liposomes can be engineered to release drugs at specific sites in the body, making them valuable carriers for targeted drug delivery.

questions.explanation: The heads (the phospho part) are polar while the tails (the lipid part) are non-polar. The heads, which form the outer and inner linings, are "hydrophilic" (water loving) while the tails that face the interior of the cell membrane are "hydrophobic" (water fearing).

questions.explanation: The largest organelle in a mature living plant cell is the central vacuole. It plays a crucial role in maintaining turgor pressure and regulating the cell's size and shape, which is essential for plant structure and growth.

questions.text: The outer and inner membranes of mitochondria spindle are?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The outer membrane is a phospholipid bilayer consisting of porins. It separates the inside organelle from the rest of the cell and primarily transports nucleotides, ions, and metabolites between cytosol and the intermembrane space. It is also associated with the endoplasmic reticulum. (The outer membrane is similar to the plasma membrane in structure). The inner membrane has several folds called cristae. It is the site of an electron transport chain and produces ATP This membrane is permeable to oxygen, carbon, dioxide, and water only and acts as an electric insulator and chemical barrier. Thus the membranes of mitochondria are structurally and functionally dissimilar.

This option is not correct. While the outer and inner membranes of mitochondria are indeed structurally different, they have distinct functions. The outer membrane is primarily involved in controlling the exchange of molecules between the mitochondria and the cytoplasm, while the inner membrane is where most of the energy production occurs. Thus, their functions are not similar.
This option is not correct. The outer and inner membranes of mitochondria are structurally and functionally different, as explained in option a).

This option is not correct. The outer and inner membranes of mitochondria are not structurally similar. The inner membrane is highly folded and contains specialized protein complexes, whereas the outer membrane is relatively smooth and contains porin proteins. Their functions are also different, as discussed in option a).

questions.explanation: The outer membrane is a phospholipid bilayer consisting of porins. It separates the inside organelle from the rest of the cell and primarily transports nucleotides, ions, and metabolites between cytosol and the intermembrane space. It is also associated with the endoplasmic reticulum. (The outer membrane is similar to the plasma membrane in structure).The inner membrane has several folds called cristae. It is the site of an electron transport chain and produces ATP. This membrane is permeable to oxygen, carbon dioxide, and water only and acts as an electric insulator and chemical barrier. Thus the membranes of mitochondria are structurally and functionally dissimilar.

questions.explanation: The cell wall of plants is composed of three main layers: the primary wall, the secondary wall, and the middle lamella. The middle lamella is first to be formed in between the primary walls of the neighboring cells. The primary wall is composed of cellulose and some deposition of pectin and hemicellulose. It is a true wall and develops in newly growing cells. The secondary wall is formed on its inner surface and is comparatively thick and rigid. The order of the cell wall layers growing inwards is: Middle lamella→Primary cell wall→Secondary cell wall.

questions.text: Which organelle was a prokaryote:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: According to endosymbiotic theory, both mitochondria and chloroplasts were prokaryotes.
According to endosymbiotic theory, both mitochondria and chloroplasts were prokaryotes.
According to endosymbiotic theory, both mitochondria and chloroplasts were prokaryotes.
According to endosymbiotic theory, both mitochondria and chloroplasts were prokaryotes so this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The mitochondrion and the chloroplast are both organelles that were once free-living cells. They were prokaryotes that ended up inside other cells (host cells). They may have joined the other cell by being eaten (a process called phagocytosis), or perhaps they were parasites of that host cell. Rather than being digested by or killing the host cell, the inner cell survived, and together they thrived. It’s kind of like a landlord and a tenant. The host cell provides a comfortable, safe place to live and the organelle pays rent by making energy that the host cell can use. This happened a long time ago, and over time the organelle and the host cell have evolved together. Now one could not exist without the other. Today they function as a single organism, but we can still find evidence of the free-living past of the organelles if we look closely.

questions.text: Blood group antigen contains:

options.explanation: Blood group antigens are typically composed of glycoproteins. Glycoproteins are molecules consisting of proteins with attached carbohydrate chains. These carbohydrate chains can determine the specific blood group antigens on the surface of red blood cells.

Phospholipids are a type of lipid that forms the major component of cell membranes. While phospholipids are important for cell structure and function, they are not directly involved in determining blood group antigens.

Glycolipids are another type of molecule consisting of lipids (fatty acids) with attached carbohydrate chains. While glycolipids are present on the surface of cell membranes, they are not primarily involved in determining blood group antigens.

Sphingolipids are a class of lipids that contain a specific sphingosine backbone. Like phospholipids and glycolipids, they are important components of cell membranes. However, they are not directly responsible for determining blood group antigens.

questions.explanation: Glycoproteins: This option is the correct answer. Blood group antigens are typically composed of glycoproteins. Glycoproteins are molecules consisting of proteins with attached carbohydrate chains. These carbohydrate chains can determine the specific blood group antigens on the surface of red blood cells.

Nucleus is the membrane-bound organelle that contains DNA and is responsible for controlling cell activities, including DNA replication, transcription (RNA synthesis), and gene expression.

questions.text: The intake of liquid materials across the cell membrane is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Phagocytosis is a process wherein a cell binds to the item it wants to engulf on the cell surface and draws the item inward while engulfing around it. It is the taking in of solid particles.
Pinocytosis refers to the uptake of extracellular fluids and dissolved solutes, such as fat droplets, vitamins, and antigens.
Endocytosis is a cellular process by which the extracellular materials or cargo are transported into intracellular compartments by a series of pathways followed by the formation of vesicles.

Exocytosis is the fusion of secretory vesicles with the plasma membrane and results in the discharge of vesicle content into the extracellular space and the incorporation of new proteins and lipids into the plasma membrane.

questions.explanation: The ingestion of liquid material into a cell by the budding of small vesicles from the cell membrane is called pinocytosis while the process by which certain living cells called phagocytes ingest or engulf other cells or solid particles is called phagocytosis.

questions.text: Organelle involved in the synthesis of ATP is
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Ribosome is an intracellular structure made of both RNA and protein, and it is the site of protein synthesis in the cell. The ribosome reads the messenger RNA (mRNA) sequence and translates that genetic code into a specified string of amino acids, which grow into long chains that fold to form proteins.

The classic role of mitochondria is oxidative phosphorylation, which generates ATP by utilizing the energy released during the oxidation of the food we eat. ATP is used in turn as the primary energy source for most biochemical and physiological processes, such as growth, movement, and homeostasis.

The nucleus is the membrane-enclosed organelle within a cell that contains the chromosomes. An array of holes, or pores, in the nuclear membrane allows for the selective passage of certain molecules (such as proteins and nucleic acids) into and out of the nucleus.

Centrioles are paired barrel-shaped organelles located in the cytoplasm of animal cells near the nuclear envelope. Centrioles play a role in organizing microtubules that serve as the cell's skeletal system. They help determine the locations of the nucleus and other organelles within the cell.

questions.explanation: The classic role of mitochondria is oxidative phosphorylation, which generates ATP by utilizing the energy released during the oxidation of the food we eat. ATP is used in turn as the primary energy source for most biochemical and physiological processes, such as growth, movement, and homeostasis.

questions.text: _is a process by which the cell takes in fluids along with dissolved small molecules. In this process, the cell membrane folds and creates small pockets and captures the cellular fluid and dissolved substances.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Pinocytosis is a process of cellular intake where the cell membrane folds inwards to form small pockets or vesicles, allowing the cell to engulf and bring in fluids along with dissolved small molecules. This process is often referred to as "cellular drinking" because it resembles the cell's way of ingesting liquids. Pinocytosis is a passive process that doesn't require energy input from the cell.
Phagocytosis, on the other hand, is a process where the cell engulfs large solid particles, such as bacteria or cellular debris. This is often referred to as cellular eating.
Transcytosis involves the transport of molecules or particles across a cell by first endocytosing them on one side of the cell and then exocytosing them on the opposite side.

Active transport is a process that requires the cell to expend energy (usually in the form of ATP) to move molecules or ions against their concentration gradient, from an area of lower concentration to an area of higher concentration.

questions.explanation: Pinocytosis is the cellular process by which a cell takes in fluids along with dissolved small molecules. It involves the folding of the cell membrane to form small pockets or vesicles. Unlike phagocytosis, pinocytosis is a passive process that does not require energy expenditure by the cell. Transcytosis involves transporting molecules across a cell, while active transport requires energy to move molecules against their concentration gradient.

questions.text: The simplest independent unit of life is known as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A bacterial colony refers to a visible cluster of bacterial cells that have grown and multiplied on a solid surface. It is not the simplest independent unit of life.
Cell is the simplest independent unit of life. All living organisms are made up of one or more cells, and cells are responsible for carrying out all the basic functions of life.

Chloroplasts are specialized structures found in plant cells and some other organisms. They are responsible for carrying out photosynthesis, the process by which plants convert sunlight into energy. It is not the simplest independent unit of life.

DNA is the general material found in all living organisms. It contains the instructions necessary for the development, functioning, and reproduction of organisms. DNA is a molecule rather than a unit of life itself, and it requires a cell to carry out its instructions.

questions.explanation: The cell is the simplest entity that can exist as an independent unit of life. Every known living organism is either a single cell or an ensemble of a few to many cells.

questions.text: The process by which unwanted structures within the cell are engulfed and digested within the lysosome is known as:

options.explanation: Endocytosis is the process by which cells take in material from outside the cell.
Exocytosis is the process by which cells release material from inside the cell.
Hydrolysis is the process of breaking down molecules into smaller molecules using water.

questions.text: The _ model of plasma membrane suggests that proteins are embedded in lipid bilayer.

options.explanation: The unit membrane does not specifically refer to the arrangement of proteins in the plasma membrane. It refers to the general structure of cellular membranes, which consists of a lipid bilayer with embedded proteins.

The fluid mosaic model of the plasma membrane suggests that proteins are embedded within a fluid lipid bilayer. According to this model, the plasma membrane is composed of a phospholipid bilayer in which various proteins are scattered or embedded, giving it a mosaic-like appearance.

Permeable refers to the property of a membrane that allows substances to pass through. While the fluid mosaic model describes the arrangement of proteins in the plasma membrane, it does not specifically refer to the permeability of the membrane.

Ultracentrifuge is a laboratory instrument used to separate and isolate cellular components based on their size, density, and other physical properties. It is not directly related to the model of the plasma membrane or the arrangement of proteins within it.

questions.explanation: According to the Fluid Mosaic Model, cell membranes are quasi-fluidic in nature with a viscous lipid bilayer having proteins at places like mosaic both on the outer surface and embedded inside. Singer and Nicolson have described the cell membrane as a 'protein iceberg' in the sea of lipids.

The function of the nucleolus is to make ribosomes. The nucleolus is a distinct subcompartment within the cell nucleus, and it plays a vital role in ribosome biogenesis, which is the process of producing ribosomes, the cellular machinery responsible for protein synthesis.

questions.explanation: The function of the nucleolus is to make ribosomes. The nucleolus is a distinct subcompartment within the cell nucleus, and it plays a vital role in ribosome biogenesis, which is the process of producing ribosomes, the cellular machinery responsible for protein synthesis. The nucleolus is responsible for making ribosomes by transcribing, processing, and assembling the components of ribosomes before exporting them to the cytoplasm, where they perform their crucial role in protein synthesis. Ribosomes are essential cellular structures involved in protein synthesis. They read the genetic information encoded in messenger RNA (mRNA) and use this information to assemble amino acids into polypeptide chains, which eventually fold into functional proteins.

questions.text: The protoplast of a plant cell does not include:

questions.text: Which interactions are responsible for the formation of lipid bilayer In the watery environment of the body?

options.text: Hydrophobic interactions between fatty acid tails
options.explanation: This option is partially correct. Hydrophobic interactions between the hydrocarbon (fatty acid) tails of phospholipids are a key driving force for the formation of the lipid bilayer. Since the interior of the lipid bilayer is hydrophobic (water-repellent), the hydrophobic tails of the phospholipids cluster together to minimize contact with water.

options.explanation: This option is not accurate. Hydrophilic interactions involve interactions between water molecules and polar or charged molecules. The fatty acid tails of phospholipids are hydrophobic and do not interact favorably with water molecules.

options.text: Hydrogen bonding between fatty acid tails
options.explanation: This option is not accurate. Hydrogen bonding involves interactions between hydrogen atoms and electronegative atoms (such as oxygen or nitrogen). Fatty acid tails are composed primarily of hydrocarbon chains, which do not readily form hydrogen bonds with each other.

questions.text: Which cell does not carry out oxidative phosphorylation?

questions.explanation: Stomach cells (option a), liver cells (option b), and pancreatic cells (option c) can all utilize the Golgi complex to form secretory granules containing digestive enzymes and other molecules. However, muscle cells (option d) are not primarily responsible for producing secretory granules. Muscle cells are specialized for contraction and movement and have different cellular functions compared to cells involved in secretion. Therefore, the Golgi complex is not responsible for the formation of secretory granules in muscle cells (option d).

questions.text: A disease caused by the absence of a lysosomal enzyme responsible for lipid catabolism:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Tay-Sachs disease is the one in which a lipid digesting enzyme is missing or inactive and the brain becomes impaired by an accumulation of lipids in the cell.

Phenylketonuria (fen-ul-key-toe-NU-ree-uh), also called PKU, is a rare inherited disorder that causes an amino acid called phenylalanine to build up in the body. PKU is caused by a change in the phenylalanine hydroxylase (PAH) gene.
Klinefelter syndrome (sometimes called Klinefelter's, KS or XXY) is where boys and men are born with an extra X chromosome.
Down syndrome is a condition in which a person has an extra chromosome.

questions.text: In mitochondria, small knob-like structures called F1 particles are found in
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The outer mitochondrial membrane is freely permeable to small molecules. This is because it contains proteins called porins, which form channels that allow the free diffusion of molecules smaller than about 6000 daltons.
The inner membrane creates two compartments. The region between the inner and outer membrane, called the intermembrane space.

The cristae bear several knob-like structures known as F1 particles or oxysomes. As per some estimates, there are 104 to 105 F1 particles per mitochondrion. However, the number of F1 particles varies with the metabolic state of a cell.
The inner cavity of the mitochondria is filled with a gel-like matrix that contains circular DNA, phosphate granules, few small-sized ribosomes. This is termed as matrix.

questions.text: Organelle involved in the synthesis of ATP is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Ribosomes are the protein factories of the cell. They are responsible for the formation of proteins by translation of messenger RNA.

Ths is the correct answer. Mitochondria are the powerhouses of the cell. They are responsible for ATP generation through cellular respiration. They contain ATP synsthase enzymes in the inner membrane at cristae. These enzymes catalyze the formation of ATP during electron transport chain(ETC).
This option is incorrect. Nucleus is the brain of cell. It contains all the genetic information of the cell in the form of DNA. It is not responsible for ATP formation.
This option is incorrect. Centrioles are the site of attachment of spindle fibres during cell division. So, they are important for cell division and are not involved in ATP synthesis.

questions.explanation: Mitochondria are the powerhouses of the cell. They are responsible for ATP generation through cellular respiration. They contain ATP synsthase enzymes in the inner membrane at cristae. These enzymes catalyze the formation of ATP during electron transport chain(ETC).

questions.text: The structure present in a eukaryotic cell but absent in prokaryotic cells is;
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: The defining difference, between eukaryotic and prokaryotic cells, is membrane-bound organelles including the nucleus. Eukaryotic cells possess membrane-bound organelles but prokaryotes do not.All other three options describe structures common to both, i.e. DNA, ribosomes (which are organelles but not membrane bound), and cell surface membrane.

questions.text: Select the wrong statement from the following.
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Both chloroplasts and mitochondria contain DNA.
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Both organelles have their own DNA, allowing them to carry out some functions independently within the cell.

options.text: The mitochondria are generally smaller than chloroplast
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Mitochondria are typically smaller than chloroplasts.

options.text: Both chloroplasts and mitochondria contain an inner and an outer membrane
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Both organelles have a double-membrane structure with an inner and an outer membrane.

options.text: Both chloroplasts and mitochondria have an internal compartment - the thylakoid space bounded by the thylakoid membrane
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. While chloroplasts have an internal compartment called the thylakoid space, mitochondria do not have a thylakoid space. The thylakoid space is where the light-dependent reactions of photosynthesis occur in chloroplasts.

questions.explanation: The statement D is incorrect. While chloroplasts have an internal compartment called the thylakoid space, mitochondria do not have a thylakoid space. The thylakoid space is where the light-dependent reactions of photosynthesis occur in chloroplasts.

This is the correct option. As muscle cells need more ATP than other cells to perform contractions, a large number of MItochondria is found in muscle cells. That is why, it is the most ideal location to study mitochondria.
The most abundant number of mitochondria is found in muscle cells. Hence this option is incorrect.
E.coli is a bacteria. Bacteria lacks membrane-bunded organelles i.e mitochondria. Hence this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Mitochondria are most abundant in muscle cells because of the strenuous metabolic activities. E.coli is a prokaryotic organism and lacks mitochondria. RBCs also lack mitochondria.

questions.text: The entire cell wall of bacteria is often regarded as a single huge molecule or molecular complex called?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: It is a polysaccharide layer that lies outside the cell envelope, and is thus deemed part of the outer envelope of a bacterial cell.
It is inner to primary cell wall in higher plants.
A slime layer is loosely associated with the bacterium and can be easily washed off.

Perhaps the most distinctive feature of the prokaryotic cell is its cell wall, composed of polysaccharide chains bound covalently to shorter chains of amino acids forming peptidoglycan or murein. The entire cell wall is often regarded as a single huge molecule or molecular complex called sacculus.

questions.explanation: In case of bacterial cells, the entire cell wall is often regarded as a single huge molecule or molecular complex known as sacculus. Capsules and slimes are outer coverings of the cell wall in some bacteria.

questions.text: A cell organelle containing hydrolytic enzymes is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Lysosomes are small vesicles which are bounded by a single membrane and contain hydrolytic enzymes. About 50 enzymes have been recorded to occur in them. The important enzymes are acid phosphatases, sulphatases, proteases, peptidases, nucleases, lipases, and carbohydrases. They are also called acid hydrolases because these digestive enzymes usually function in acidic medium of pH of 4­-5 and help in intracellular digestion.
Microbodies include peroxisomes and glyoxysomes. Peroxisomes are responsible for detoxification of alcohols and contain oxidative enzymes. Glyoxysomes contain enzymes for plant lipid metabolism.
Ribosomes are responsible for protein synthesis. They donot contain hydrolytic enzymes.
These are invagination of plasma membrane in prokaryotes. These help in DNA replication and respiration.

The important enzymes are acid phosphatases, sulphatases, proteases, peptidases, nucleases, lipases, and carbohydrases. They are also called acid hydrolases because these digestive enzymes usually function in acidic medium of pH of 4­-5 and help in intracellular digestion. Microtubules are unbranched, hollow, submicroscopic tubules of the protein tubulin. In plant cell microtubules organize themselves to form spindle fibres. In animal cell , microtubules are involved in formation of centrioles, cilia,flagella and basal body.

questions.text: Which of the following structures is not found in a prokaryotic cell?

options.explanation: Mesosomes are invagination of plasma membrane found in prokaryotes.
Prokaryotes have plasma membrane and cell wall as well.

A prokaryotic cell is characterised by absence of an organised nucleus and membrane bound cell organelles. DNA is naked i.e., without a nuclear envelope and coiled in the cytoplasm. It is commonly called nucleoid or genophore.
Prokaryotes have 70S ribosomes found in matrix of mitochondria and stroma of chloroplast.

questions.text: Select the correct matching in the following pairs.

options.text: Rough ER – Oxidation of fatty acids
options.explanation: Rough ER is involved in protein synthesis.
Smooth endoplasmic reticulum is involved in synthesis of lipids and metabolism of carbohydrates.

Smooth endoplasmic reticulum (SER) is a system of smooth membranes (i.e., membranes not having ribosomes) within the cytoplasm of plant and animal cells. It is the site of important metabolic reactions, including phospholipid and fatty acid synthesis. In animal cells lipid-­like steroidal hormones are also synthesized.

questions.text: The solid linear cytoskeletal elements having a diameter of 6 nm and made up of a single type of monomer are known as
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Microtubules are hollow microscopic tubular structures with an external diameter of 24 nm and of variable length.
Microfilaments are fine, thread-like protein fibers, 3-6 nm in diameter. They are composed predominantly of a contractile protein called actin.

Intermediate filaments are the numerous microscopic protein fibers of about 10 nm thickness that form part of the cytoskeleton.They are intermediate in size between microtubules and microfilaments.They are made up of a variety of proteins e.g. keratin in nails.
Cilia are formed of microtubules which are 24 nm in diameter.

questions.text: The osmotic expansion of a cell kept in water is chiefly regulated by

Plastids are responsible for photosynthesis and food storage.
Ribosomes are involved in protein synthesis.

questions.text: The Golgi complex plays a major role
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Golgi complex does not transfer energy.

options.text: in post translational modification of proteins and glycosylation of lipids
options.explanation: Post translational modification (PTM) is a step in protein biosynthesis. Proteins are synthesized in ribosomes translating mRNA into polypeptide chains. These polypeptide chains undergo PTM, such as folding, cutting, and other processes, before becoming the mature protein product. Proteins synthesized by the rough endoplasmic reticulum and lipids synthesized by smooth endoplasmic reticulum reach the cisternae of the Golgi apparatus. Here, they combine with carbohydrates to form glycoproteins and glycolipids. This process is called glycosylation.

options.text: in trapping the light and transforming it nto chemical energy
options.explanation: This is the role of chlorophyll present in chloroplast.
Intracellular digestion is the role of lysosomes.
